options.text: Simulated data is too expensive to generate compared to real-world data.
AI models trained on clean simulations often fail when deployed in the messy real world.
Virtual worlds are physically impossible for computers to render accurately.

exercises.explain: Simulating environments is cheap and safe, but AI models trained only on virtual worlds often fail in the messy, complex real world.
exercises.prompt: Why can't we just train self-driving cars entirely inside basic video game simulations?

cards.title: The Reality Gap
cards.body: Imagine training a robot to grasp objects using billions of simulated attempts. It works perfectly in the virtual simulator, but the moment you put it in a real room, it freezes. Why? Because the real world has shadows, dust, and textures that the simulator never prepared it for!

The Sim-to-Real Bridge uses GANs to translate artificial, simulated images into photorealistic ones. This process (a form of domain adaptation) allows us to train AI models on virtually infinite simulated data, then modify that data so it behaves and looks just like the real world.
cards.title: Crossing the Bridge

cards.body: Think of a simulator as a coloring book with simple black-and-white outlines. Training an AI on this directly is like teaching someone to recognize animals using only outlines. A Sim-to-Real GAN acts like an expert artist who colors in the outlines with realistic fur textures, lighting, and shading so the AI learns what real animals actually look like.
cards.title: The Coloring Book Artist
Upgrading Virtual Streets
cards.body: A self-driving car simulator generates a 3D street scene with flat, gray asphalt and blocky buildings. The Sim-to-Real GAN processes this scene, adding realistic asphalt cracks, wet reflections from rain, and complex brick textures, preparing the car's camera systems for actual road conditions.

Many believe GANs are only used to create fake human faces or art. In reality, Sim-to-Real GANs are critical engineering tools used by robotics and autonomous vehicle companies to make simulation training transfer safely to physical hardware.
cards.title: Not Just for Art

left.text: Flat textures, perfect lighting, clean geometry, no lens noise.

right.text: Detailed textures, realistic shadows, lens glare, and dirt.

cards.body: See how a GAN bridges the gap by adding realistic details to simple simulated environments.
cards.title: Simulation vs. Reality

exercises.prompt: How does a Sim-to-Real GAN assist in domain adaptation?
exercises.explain: Domain adaptation helps transfer knowledge learned in one domain (like virtual simulations) to another domain (like the real physical world).

options.text: It translates simulated images into photorealistic ones so models transfer better to real environments.
It deletes simulated data to make room for manual real-world photographs.
It makes real-world cameras record images in a cartoonish, simulated style.

exercises.explain: Sim-to-Real GANs modify the visual appearance of simulated data to match real-world distributions, keeping the underlying labels (like object positions) intact.
exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a Sim-to-Real GAN allows developers to leverage cheap, labeled simulation data while preparing the AI for real-world environments.

exercises.explain: By transforming simulated images to look photorealistic, we can train models on simulated data that transfers successfully to real environments.
exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of Sim-to-Real.
exercises.sentence: A Sim-to-Real GAN translates _______ images to photorealistic ones to help train AI models safely.

options.text: A virtual 3D city block gets enhanced with realistic wet pavement reflections and camera sensor noise.
A camera records a real street and saves it directly to a computer hard drive.

exercises.explain: Applying realistic textures, lighting, and noise to synthetic renders is a hallmark task of Sim-to-Real generative models.
exercises.prompt: Identify the scenario that uses a Sim-to-Real generative system:

exercises.explain: The gap between simulated physics/visuals and the real world is known as the reality gap. Bridging it ensures models trained in simulation work in real life. Next, we will explore CycleGANs and how they translate images without paired datasets.

options.text: To bridge the reality gap so models trained in simulation can work in the real world.
To replace all physical robots with purely virtual video game characters.
To reduce the resolution of real photos so they fit on smaller memory cards.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of applying a Sim-to-Real GAN to training datasets?

title: The Sim-to-Real Bridge
concept: GANs can translate simulated images to photorealistic images (domain adaptation), helping train models on simulated data that transfers to real environments.
